ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thunder Tea Rice (lei cha fan). Mapped to the closest parent 'rice dish' class (FOODON_03311069) as the most defensible FoodOn anchor. A specific term for Hakka lei cha fan is absent from the ontology as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Thunder Tea Rice. A0CKP (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible parent category match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Thunder Tea Rice or lei cha fan as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Thunder Tea Rice is approximately 350 g, comprising a bowl of rice topped with assorted blanched and stir-fried vegetables, tofu, peanuts, sesame seeds, and fried anchovies, accompanied by a separate bowl of warm green herbal tea soup.

nutrient_source.title: Thunder Tea Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Thunder Tea Rice (F05100146). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this primary regional source. Sugar value is reported as -1 (not available) in the HPB source record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; mixed rice dish with vegetables analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thunder Tea Rice (lei cha fan) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 54 based on the closest analogous entry: mixed rice dish with vegetables and legumes (GI ~50–58, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The high fibre content (3.6 g/100 g) and protein (6.2 g/100 g) from tofu and anchovies are expected to moderate the glycaemic response relative to plain white rice (GI ~72). GL per serving reported as 10.2 based on a 140 g rice-equivalent portion: (54 × 13.5 × 1.4) / 100 = 10.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 312 mg/100 g; high-sodium toppings (preserved radish, dried shrimp, fried anchovies)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced quantities of preserved radish (chai poh), dried shrimp, and fried anchovies, as these toppings are the primary sodium contributors; the vegetable and legume base is DASH-compatible and the estimated potassium at ~220 mg/100 g provides partial offset, but total sodium per 350 g serving (~1092 mg) approaches the 1500 mg daily target for hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate density (13.5 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (3.6 g/100 g), moderate protein (6.2 g/100 g) moderating glycaemic response; estimated GI 54 (low)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thunder Tea Rice is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate density, high fibre content exceeding the 3 g/100 g threshold, and moderate protein from tofu and anchovies that collectively moderate the glycaemic response; patients should request brown rice to further reduce GI and monitor total portion size to manage overall carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.5 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (3.6 g/100 g), green tea polyphenols, plant sterols from peanuts and sesame; sodium at 312 mg/100 g requires monitoring
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Thunder Tea Rice has a favourable cardiovascular profile due to low saturated fat, high fibre, and cardioprotective phytonutrients from green tea, peanuts, and sesame seeds; however, patients with cardiovascular disease should request reduced high-sodium toppings (preserved radish, dried shrimp, fried anchovies) to keep total sodium within the 1500–2000 mg/day clinical target.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory phytonutrients: green tea catechins, vitamin E from peanuts and sesame, antioxidants from leafy greens; low saturated fat (~1.5 g/100 g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Thunder Tea Rice is well-suited for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its multiple anti-inflammatory components including green tea polyphenols, vitamin E-rich peanuts and sesame seeds, and antioxidant-rich leafy greens; patients should request reduced preserved radish and dried shrimp to minimise sodium-driven joint fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thunder Tea Rice (lei cha fan; 擂茶饭) is a traditional Hakka Chinese dish widely popular in Singapore, particularly among the Hakka community. It consists of a bowl of steamed white rice surrounded by small portions of blanched and stir-fried vegetables — typically long beans, cabbage, spinach or kai lan — alongside cubed tofu, dried shrimp, preserved radish (chai poh), roasted peanuts, sesame seeds, and crispy fried anchovies (ikan bilis). The dish is served with a separate bowl of warm green herbal soup made by grinding or pounding fresh green tea leaves, basil, mint, peppercorns, and other herbs with water. Diners pour the herbal soup over the rice and toppings before eating. The flavour profile is earthy, slightly bitter from the green tea, nutty from peanuts and sesame, and savoury from the dried seafood toppings. The texture is varied — soft rice, crunchy peanuts, tender vegetables, and crispy anchovies. Nutritionally, Thunder Tea Rice is notable for its relatively low calorie density (150 kcal/100 g), moderate protein (6.2 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (3.6 g/100 g), and meaningful micronutrient content including calcium (49 mg/100 g) and iron (0.94 mg/100 g). The dish is classified as vegetarian-adjacent but typically contains dried shrimp and anchovies; fully vegetarian versions exist. Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to low carbohydrate density and high fibre, and supports cardiovascular health through its anti-inflammatory phytonutrients. Sodium at 312 mg/100 g is the primary concern for hypertensive individuals, driven by preserved and dried seafood toppings. The dish is a staple at Hakka hawker stalls and vegetarian eateries across Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05100146), which is the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. The match is exact. GI is estimated from the closest analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database (mixed rice dish with vegetables and legumes) due to absence of a direct measurement for lei cha fan; this reduces GI confidence to medium but does not affect overall record confidence given the strong primary nutrient source.
notes: 1. sugar_g is set to -1: the HPB Singapore Foodo source record for Thunder Tea Rice (F05100146) does not report a sugar value; detailed sugar breakdown is not available in the HPB database entry for this dish as of 2026-04-16. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Thunder Tea Rice is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and water content data beyond calcium and iron are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this dish; no approved secondary source provides a sufficiently close match for these values. 4. saturated_fat_g (1.5 g/100 g) and potassium_mg (~220 mg/100 g) are estimated from the dish composition (peanuts, sesame, tofu, anchovies) using USDA FoodData Central component data as a secondary reference; these are approximate and should be verified against a direct HPB fatty acid report if available. 5. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Thunder Tea Rice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 6. is_halal set to false as the standard preparation includes dried shrimp and fried anchovies which may not be halal-certified; halal-certified versions are available at some stalls. 7. is_vegetarian set to false as standard preparation includes dried shrimp and fried anchovies; fully vegetarian versions exist and are common at vegetarian hawker stalls. 8. Ontology: foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' per allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']; both FoodOn (FOODON_03311069, rice dish) and FoodEx2 (A0CKP, rice-based composite dish) are closest parent matches as no specific term for Thunder Tea Rice exists in either ontology as of 2024.
